title: Exploring Earable-Based Passive User Authentication via Interpretable In-Ear Breathing Biometrics

method.input_representation: MFCCs, Cross-Power Spectral Density (CPSD), Power Cumulative Distribution
method.training_strategy: Triplet loss with data augmentation (amplitude scaling, time stretch, Gaussian noise, pitch shift, time shift)

evaluation.context: Achieved under random attacks for fast breathing

evaluation_protocol: Cross-validation using 20% of samples for training and 80% for authentication; tested across 4 daily environments

reported_limitations: Performance drops significantly during strenuous exercise (9km/h treadmill)
Ineffective for users with respiratory diseases (cold, chronic rhinitis, COPD)
Vulnerable to sound interference from music played by the same earphones

usability.usability_issues: Sensitivity to physiological state (respiratory health) and physical intensity (exercise)

key_contribution: Proposed BreathSign, a passive authentication system using in-ear breathing sounds
Developed a Triplet network to extract breathing behavior-independent features
Introduced an online template update mechanism for temporal stability
main_strength: High resistance to replay attacks due to bone-conduction path
Robustness to daily ambient noise and movement
main_weakness: Failure in the presence of respiratory infections or chronic diseases
Lack of solution for on-device music interference
insights.notes_for_review: The system demonstrates strong potential for continuous passive authentication but highlights a significant 'medical gap' where physiological changes (disease) invalidate the biometric template.
